metadata.summary: A Jupyter server extension to run additional processes and proxy to them that comes bundled JupyterLab extension to launch pre-defined processes.

documentation:
# Jupyter Server Proxy

[![ReadTheDocs badge](https://img.shields.io/readthedocs/jupyter-server-proxy?logo=read-the-docs)](https://jupyter-server-proxy.readthedocs.io/)
[![GitHub Workflow Status](https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy/test.yaml?logo=github&branch=main)](https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy/actions)
[![PyPI badge](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/jupyter-server-proxy.svg?logo=pypi)](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/jupyter-server-proxy)
[![Conda badge](https://img.shields.io/conda/vn/conda-forge/jupyter-server-proxy?logo=conda-forge)](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/jupyter-server-proxy)
[![NPM badge](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy.svg?logo=npm)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy)

Jupyter Server Proxy lets you run arbitrary external processes (such as
RStudio, Shiny Server, Syncthing, PostgreSQL, Code Server, etc)
alongside your notebook server and provide authenticated web access to
them using a path like `/rstudio` next to others like `/lab`. Alongside
the python package that provides the main functionality, the JupyterLab
extension (`@jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy`) provides buttons in the
JupyterLab launcher window to get to RStudio for example.

**Note:** This project used to be called **nbserverproxy**. As
nbserverproxy is an older version of jupyter-server-proxy, uninstall
nbserverproxy before installing jupyter-server-proxy to avoid conflicts.

The primary use cases are:

1.  Use with JupyterHub / Binder to allow launching users into web
    interfaces that have nothing to do with Jupyter - such as RStudio,
    Shiny, or OpenRefine.
2.  Allow access from frontend javascript (in classic notebook or
    JupyterLab extensions) to access web APIs of other processes running
    locally in a safe manner. This is used by the [JupyterLab
    extension](https://github.com/dask/dask-labextension) for
    [dask](https://www.dask.org/).

[The documentation](https://jupyter-server-proxy.readthedocs.io/)
contains information on installation & usage.

## Security warning

Jupyter Server Proxy is often used to start a user defined process listening to
some network port (e.g. `http://localhost:4567`) for a user starting a Jupyter Server
that only that user has permission to access. The user can then access the
started process proxied through the Jupyter Server.

For safe use of Jupyter Server Proxy, you should ensure that the process started
by Jupyter Server proxy can't be accessed directly by another user and bypass
the Jupyter Server's authorization!

A common strategy to enforce access proxied via Jupyter Server is to start
Jupyter Server within a container and only allow network access to the Jupyter
Server via the container.

> For more insights, see [Ryan Lovett's comment about
> it](https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy/pull/359#issuecomment-1350118197).

## Install

### Python package

#### `pip`

```bash
pip install jupyter-server-proxy
```

#### `conda`

```bash
conda install -c conda-forge jupyter-server-proxy
```

### Jupyter Client Extensions

A JupyterLab and Notebook extension is bundled with the Python package to
provide:

- servers in the _New_ dropwdown of the Notebook Tree view
- launch buttons in JupyterLab's Launcher panel for registered server processes.
  - ![a screenshot of the JupyterLab Launcher](docs/source/_static/images/labextension-launcher.png "launch proxied servers as JupyterLab panels or new browser tabs")

#### Client compatibility

For historical compatibility ranges, see the table below:

| `jupyter-server-proxy` | `notebook` | `jupyterlab` |
| :--------------------: | :--------: | :----------: |
|        `4.1.x`         |  `>=6,<8`  |   `>=3,<5`   |
|        `4.0.x`         |  `>=6,<7`  |   `>=3,<4`   |
|         `3.x`          |  `>=6,<7`  |   `>=2,<4`   |

## Disable

### Server extension

```bash
jupyter serverextension disable --sys-prefix jupyter_server_proxy
jupyter server extension disable --sys-prefix jupyter_server_proxy
```

### Notebook Classic extension

```bash
jupyter nbextension disable --sys-prefix --py jupyter_server_proxy
```

### JupyterLab extension

```bash
jupyter labextension disable @jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy
```

#### Local development

To setup a local development environment, see the [contributing guide](https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md).

modules.name: jupyter_server_proxy.websocket
documentation:
Authenticated HTTP proxy for Jupyter Notebooks

Some original inspiration from https://github.com/senko/tornado-proxy

functions.name: jupyter_server_proxy.websocket.pingable_ws_connect
documentation:
A variation on websocket_connect that returns a PingableWSClientConnection
with on_ping_callback.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.websocket.PingableWSClientConnection
documentation: A WebSocketClientConnection with an on_ping callback.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.websocket.WebSocketHandlerMixin
documentation: from https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38663666/how-can-i-serve-a-http-page-and-a-websocket-on-the-same-url-in-tornado

functions.name: jupyter_server_proxy._jupyter_server_extension_points
documentation: Jupyter Extension points

functions.name: jupyter_server_proxy.utils.call_with_asked_args
documentation:
Call callback with only the args it wants from args

Example
\>>> def cb(a):
...    return a * 5

```pycon
>>> print(call_with_asked_args(cb, {'a': 4, 'b': 8}))
20
```

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.utils.Callable
documentation:
A trait which is callable.
Notes
-----
Classes are callable, as are instances
with a __call__() method.
copy-pasted from the ipython/traitlets source code, see
https://github.com/ipython/traitlets/blob/a1425327460c4a3ae970aeaef17e0c22da4c53c6/traitlets/traitlets.py#L3232-L3246

modules.name: jupyter_server_proxy._version
documentation: __version__ should be updated using tbump, based on configuration in
pyproject.toml, according to instructions in RELEASE.md.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.api.IconHandler

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.api.IconHandler.initialize
documentation: icons is a dict of titles to paths
Serve launcher icons
FIXME: Should be a StaticFileHandler subclass

modules.name: jupyter_server_proxy.rawsocket
documentation:
A simple translation layer between tornado websockets and asyncio stream
connections.

This provides similar functionality to websockify
(https://github.com/novnc/websockify) without needing an extra proxy hop
or process through with all messages pass for translation.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.rawsocket.RawSocketProtocol
documentation:
A protocol handler for the proxied stream connection.
Sends any received blocks directly as websocket messages.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.rawsocket.RawSocketHandler

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.rawsocket.RawSocketHandler.proxy_open
documentation:
Open the backend connection. host and port are ignored (as they are in
the parent for unix sockets) since they are always passed known values.

HTTP handler that proxies websocket connections into a backend stream.
All other HTTP requests return 405.

modules.name: jupyter_server_proxy.config
documentation: Traitlets based configuration for jupyter_server_proxy

functions.name: jupyter_server_proxy.config._make_proxy_handler
documentation: Create an appropriate handler with given parameters

functions.name: jupyter_server_proxy.config.make_handlers
documentation: Get tornado handlers for registered server_processes

modules.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers
documentation:
Authenticated HTTP proxy for Jupyter Notebooks

Some original inspiration from https://github.com/senko/tornado-proxy

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.RewritableResponse

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.RewritableResponse._apply_to_copy
documentation: Apply a function to a copy of self, and return the copy
A class to hold the response to be rewritten by rewrite_response

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.AddSlashHandler
documentation: Add trailing slash to URLs that need them.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.check_origin
documentation: Support/use jupyter_server config arguments allow_origin and allow_origin_pat
to enable cross origin requests propagated by e.g. inverting proxies.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.open
documentation: Support all the methods that tornado does by default except for GET which
is passed to WebSocketHandlerMixin and then to WebSocketHandler.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.prepare
documentation:
Enforce authentication on *all* requests.

This method is called *before* any other method for all requests.
See https://www.tornadoweb.org/en/stable/web.html#tornado.web.RequestHandler.prepare.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.http_get
documentation: Our non-websocket GET.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.on_message
documentation:
Called when we receive a message from our client.

We proxy it to the backend.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.on_ping
documentation:
Called when the client pings our websocket connection.

We proxy it to the backend.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.on_pong
documentation: Called when we receive a ping back.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.on_close
documentation:
Called when the client closes our websocket connection.

We close our connection to the backend too.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler._record_activity
documentation:
Record proxied activity as API activity

avoids proxied traffic being ignored by the notebook's
internal idle-shutdown mechanism

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler._get_context_path
jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.proxy
jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.proxy_open
documentation:
Called when a client opens a websocket connection.

We establish a websocket connection to the proxied backend &
set up a callback to relay messages through.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.proxy_request_headers
documentation:
A dictionary of headers to be used when constructing
a tornado.httpclient.HTTPRequest instance for the proxy request.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.get_request_headers_override
documentation: Add additional request headers. Typically overridden in subclasses.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.proxy_request_options
documentation:
A dictionary of options to be used when constructing
a tornado.httpclient.HTTPRequest instance for the proxy request.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.check_xsrf_cookie
documentation:
http://www.tornadoweb.org/en/stable/guide/security.html

Defer to proxied apps.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.select_subprotocol
documentation:
Select a single Sec-WebSocket-Protocol during handshake.

Overrides `tornado.websocket.WebSocketHandler.select_subprotocol` that
includes an informative docstring:
https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/v6.4.0/tornado/websocket.py#L337-L360.

class_variables.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.ProxyHandler.unix_socket
documentation: Used in subclasses

A tornado request handler that proxies HTTP and websockets from
a given host/port combination. This class is not meant to be
used directly as a means of overriding CORS. This presents significant
security risks, and could allow arbitrary remote code access. Instead, it is
meant to be subclassed and used for proxying URLs from trusted sources.

Subclasses should implement open, http_get, post, put, delete, head, patch,
and options.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.LocalProxyHandler
documentation:
A tornado request handler that proxies HTTP and websockets
from a port on the local system. Same as the above ProxyHandler,
but specific to 'localhost'.

The arguments "port" and "proxied_path" in each method are extracted from
the URL as capture groups in the regex specified in the add_handlers
method.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.RemoteProxyHandler
documentation:
A tornado request handler that proxies HTTP and websockets
from a port on a specified remote system.

The arguments "host", "port" and "proxied_path" in each method are
extracted from the URL as capture groups in the regex specified in the
add_handlers method.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.NamedLocalProxyHandler

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.NamedLocalProxyHandler._realize_rendered_template
documentation: Call any callables, then render any templated values.

A tornado request handler that proxies HTTP and websockets from a port on
the local system. The port is specified in config, and associated with a
name which forms part of the URL.

Config will create a subclass of this for each named proxy. A further
subclass below is used for named proxies where we also start the server.

classes.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.SuperviseAndProxyHandler

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.SuperviseAndProxyHandler.port
documentation:
Allocate either the requested port or a random empty port for use by
application

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.SuperviseAndProxyHandler.get_cwd
documentation:
Get the current working directory for our process

Override in subclass to launch the process in a directory
other than the current.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.SuperviseAndProxyHandler.get_env
documentation:
Set up extra environment variables for process. Typically
overridden in subclasses.

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.SuperviseAndProxyHandler.get_timeout
documentation: Return timeout (in s) to wait before giving up on process readiness

methods.name: jupyter_server_proxy.handlers.SuperviseAndProxyHandler.ensure_process
documentation: Start the process

A tornado request handler that proxies HTTP and websockets from a local
process which is launched on demand to handle requests. The command and
other process options are specified in config.

A subclass of this will be made for each configured server process.
FIXME: Move this to its own file. Too many packages now import this from nbrserverproxy.handlers
